body: Pip was a small sparrow who lived in an old orchard. Each morning brought familiar work, but Pip hoped to build a safe nest and become worthy of the trust that others had placed nearby.

One day, an easier path appeared after a promise was made. The event created a new task: keep the promise despite the delay. Turning back would have been easy, yet the choice would have affected more than Pip alone.

The first signal remains ambiguous. These minor choices matter cumulatively. The broader implication reaches beyond the immediate example. Context can mediate the effect of the journey. The outcome is contingent on conditions that may change.
Pip first tried the quickest answer. It failed because the situation demanded attention rather than speed. Then a friend carrying a lantern appeared and asked what had already been tried.

Together they studied the place, the available tools, and the time that remained. Pip began to understand that understand that trust grows through action. The next attempt was slower, but every step answered a real part of the problem.

Before the task was complete, another difficulty appeared. Pip felt the pull of an easier choice and remembered the original goal. That moment, rather than the final success, became the true test of cooperation.

By evening, the work was finished. The people who had been waiting saw only the result, while Pip remembered the uncertainty behind it. Praise mattered less than the knowledge that the decision could be explained honestly.

Years later, the story was still told in an old orchard. Its lesson was not that every obstacle has a perfect answer. It was that character becomes visible when convenience, fear, and responsibility point in different directions.
Small differences can give rise to a larger effect.

Together they studied the place, the available tools, and the time that remained. Rowan began to understand that let curiosity ask questions before it takes risks. The next attempt was slower, but every step answered a real part of the problem.

Before the task was complete, another difficulty appeared. Rowan felt the pull of an easier choice and remembered the original goal. That moment, rather than the final success, became the true test of honesty.

By evening, the work was finished. The people who had been waiting saw only the result, while Rowan remembered the uncertainty behind it. Praise mattered less than the knowledge that the decision could be explained honestly.

Years later, the story was still told in a green forest. Its lesson was not that every obstacle has a perfect answer. It was that character becomes visible when convenience, fear, and responsibility point in different directions.

articles.title: The Open Window
articles.levelFocus: tone, unreliable narration, and a twist ending
articles.dek: A nervous visitor meets a young storyteller whose calm invention transforms an ordinary room.
articles.sourceName: Project Gutenberg: Beasts and Super-Beasts by Saki
articles.adaptationNote: A C1 retelling of Saki's public-domain short story, preserving the complete plot and its dry humor.
body: Framton Nuttel had come to the countryside because his doctor had prescribed complete rest. Unfortunately, rest did not come naturally to him. Even a polite visit to a stranger could fill him with anxiety. His sister, who had once lived in the area, had given him several letters of introduction. Without them, she warned, he might spend every day alone and become even more nervous.

On his first afternoon, Framton called on Mrs Sappleton. While he waited for her, he was received by her fifteen-year-old niece, Vera. She asked whether he knew many people nearby. Framton admitted that he knew almost no one and had only his sister's letters to guide him. This information seemed to put him at ease, but it gave Vera everything she needed.

The room looked across a wide lawn, and a tall French window stood open despite the cool autumn air. Vera noticed Framton looking at it. With perfect composure, she explained that her aunt kept the window open because she expected her husband and two brothers to return through it. Three years earlier, Vera said, the men had crossed the lawn to go hunting and had disappeared into a dangerous marsh. Their bodies were never found. Mrs Sappleton still believed that they would come home one evening with their dog beside them.

Vera's account was so plausible that the open window changed from an architectural detail into a wound in the room. Framton did not know where to look. When Mrs Sappleton entered and cheerfully mentioned the hunters, he assumed grief had disturbed her mind. She apologized for the open window and said that the men would soon be back for tea.

Framton attempted to discuss his health, but Mrs Sappleton's eyes repeatedly moved toward the lawn. Then her face brightened. "Here they are at last," she said. Framton turned to Vera, expecting to see concern. Instead, the girl stared through the window with apparent horror.

Three figures were walking across the fading grass. One carried a white coat over his arm, and a tired brown dog followed them. A voice called from outside. For Framton, the scene confirmed every detail of Vera's story. He seized his hat and left abruptly, nearly colliding with a cyclist at the gate.

The hunters entered the room and asked why the visitor had fled. Mrs Sappleton complained that he had spoken of nothing but illness and had vanished without saying goodbye. Vera offered an explanation immediately. Framton, she said, was terrified of dogs because a pack of wild animals had once chased him into a graveyard beside the Ganges.

No one questioned her. The story matched the frightened departure closely enough to satisfy the room. Creating dramatic explanations at short notice was Vera's specialty. Her mischief depended not on an impossible lie, but on a precise improvisation: she observed what people feared, then gave that fear a convincing shape.

vocabulary.definition: a calm and controlled state, especially in a difficult situation
vocabulary.note: Vera 讲述悲剧时异常镇定，使谎言更可信。
vocabulary.example: She maintained her composure during the interview.
vocabulary.chinese: Vera 讲述悲剧时异常镇定，使谎言更可信。
vocabulary.usage: 结合正文中的语境和上方例句使用，注意词性及常见搭配。

vocabulary.note: 故事并非真实，但细节让它显得可信。
vocabulary.example: The witness gave a plausible explanation.

vocabulary.usage: 结合正文中的语境和上方例句使用，注意词性及常见搭配。

articles.title: Tomas and the Abandoned Garden
articles.levelFocus: nuanced argument, flexible syntax, and precise abstract language
articles.dek: A graded C1 story about accuracy, choice, and the abandoned garden.
articles.sourceName: English Reading Lab original story collection
articles.adaptationNote: An original C1 story written for this reading collection.
body: Tomas was a mapmaker's apprentice who lived in a windy harbor. Each morning brought familiar work, but Tomas hoped to draw an accurate coast and become worthy of the trust that others had placed nearby.

One day, an untended garden was drying behind a locked gate. The event created a new task: save the plants without damaging the property. Turning back would have been easy, yet the choice would have affected more than Tomas alone.

The broader implication reaches beyond the immediate example. Context can mediate the effect of the journey. The outcome is contingent on conditions that may change. A strong solution must reconcile competing needs. An underlying pattern connects the visible details.
Tomas first tried the quickest answer. It failed because the situation demanded attention rather than speed. Then a retired groundskeeper appeared and asked what had already been tried.

Together they studied the place, the available tools, and the time that remained. Tomas began to understand that care for a place while respecting who is responsible for it. The next attempt was slower, but every step answered a real part of the problem.

Before the task was complete, another difficulty appeared. Tomas felt the pull of an easier choice and remembered the original goal. That moment, rather than the final success, became the true test of accuracy.

By evening, the work was finished. The people who had been waiting saw only the result, while Tomas remembered the uncertainty behind it. Praise mattered less than the knowledge that the decision could be explained honestly.

Years later, the story was still told in a windy harbor. Its lesson was not that every obstacle has a perfect answer. It was that character becomes visible when convenience, fear, and responsibility point in different directions.
The outcome may be contingent on conditions that remain uncertain.
What appears to be a small detail can shape the whole experience.
Beneath the visible surface, the journey keeps a second history.

articles.title: A Butterfly's Four Stages
articles.levelFocus: very common words, short sentences, and concrete meaning
articles.dek: A A1 science explainer about butterfly development, adaptation, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A1.
body: A Butterfly's Four Stages is the topic of this article. We can study it on plants and in the air. Conditions can change. The system must respond in a new way.
The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then an egg becomes a caterpillar, pupa, and adult.

The result is easy to see. This is a useful part of this natural system. People compare one part of this natural system with another. There is a simple reason for this. People often see this in this natural system.
This means that one animal takes several very different forms.
A problem can appear because weather and food affect survival. Scientists watch these changes.
The important details appear one by one.
This natural system changes over time.
This natural system is like a small map.

vocabulary.definition: what happens because of an action
vocabulary.example: The result of the test was clear.
vocabulary.usage: 常见搭配是 the result of 和 as a result。

vocabulary.definition: helping you do or understand something
vocabulary.example: This map is useful for visitors.
vocabulary.usage: 常用 useful for + 名词或 useful to + 人。

vocabulary.definition: to look at two things to see how they are similar or different
vocabulary.example: Compare the two pictures.
vocabulary.usage: compare A with B 表示比较两者。

vocabulary.example: There is a reason for the change.
vocabulary.usage: 常用 the reason for 或 the reason why。

vocabulary.example: We often walk after dinner.
vocabulary.usage: 放在一般动词前、be 动词后。

vocabulary.definition: something difficult that needs an answer
vocabulary.example: We have a small problem.
vocabulary.usage: solve a problem 表示“解决问题”。

vocabulary.definition: not large
vocabulary.example: A small change can help.
vocabulary.usage: 可修饰具体事物或抽象变化。

vocabulary.example: People share the road.
vocabulary.usage: people 本身就是复数含义。

vocabulary.example: This takes time.

phrases.usage: 描述人或事物依次发生。

articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A1.
body: From Seed to Young Plant is the topic of this article. We can study it inside moist soil. A movement or change can be a signal. It gives information about the system.
The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then water wakes the seed and a root begins to grow.

A clear pattern appears in this natural system. This natural system changes slowly over time. The result is easy to see. This is a useful part of this natural system. People compare one part of this natural system with another.
This means that a shoot reaches upward toward light.
A problem can appear because too little water or warmth can stop growth. Scientists watch these changes.
In the end, a careful observer understands this natural system more clearly.
This natural system changes over time.
This natural system is like a small map.

The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then water vapor cools and gathers around tiny particles.

A careful observer is careful before making a choice. A careful observer can notice a small change. A clear pattern appears in this natural system. This natural system changes slowly over time. The result is easy to see.

A problem can appear because wind and temperature keep changing them. Scientists watch these changes.

The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then an invisible magnetic field produces a push or pull.

People compare one part of this natural system with another. There is a simple reason for this. People often see this in this natural system. One problem can change what happens next. A careful observer is careful before making a choice.
This means that objects can move without direct contact.

This means that places move into light and then into darkness.

body: Why Shadows Move is the topic of this article. We can study it where sunlight meets solid objects. The steps happen in an order. One step helps the next step begin.
The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then light travels in straight lines and an object blocks part of it.

A careful observer is careful before making a choice. A careful observer can notice a small change. A clear pattern appears in this natural system. This natural system changes slowly over time. The result is easy to see.
This means that a dark shape appears on the other side.
A problem can appear because clouds and several lights can change the shape. Scientists watch these changes.
At first, a careful observer sees only one part of this natural system.
This natural system changes over time.

body: Why Teeth Need Cleaning is the topic of this article. We can study it inside the mouth after meals. A movement or change can be a signal. It gives information about the system.
The system uses energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Then bacteria use food left on teeth and produce acids.

This means that cleaning removes material before it causes damage.
A problem can appear because hard-to-reach places can keep food longer. Scientists watch these changes.
In the end, a careful observer understands this natural system more clearly.

vocabulary.usage: over time 表示“随着时间”。

phrases.definition: finally, after a period of time
phrases.example: In the end, a careful observer understands this natural system more clearly.
phrases.usage: 表示经过一段过程后的结果。

phrases.definition: across a period, not all at once
phrases.example: Small changes appear over time.
phrases.usage: 常用于描述慢慢发生的变化。

grammar.target: This natural system changes over time.
grammar.definition: The present simple describes a fact or repeated process.
grammar.note: 一般现在时用于说明事实、规律或经常发生的动作。
grammar.example: Water freezes at zero degrees.

grammar.target: Why Teeth Need Cleaning is the topic of this article.
grammar.definition: The present simple states a fact, habit, or stable situation.
grammar.note: 一般现在时用来说明事实、状态或经常发生的动作。
grammar.example: The town needs clean water.

rhetoric.target: This natural system is like a small map.
rhetoric.definition: A simile compares two things using like.
rhetoric.note: 用 like 把主题比作地图，帮助读者形成简单画面。
rhetoric.effect: 把抽象过程变成容易理解的具体物体。
rhetoric.tryIt: A timetable is like a map of the day.

rhetoric.target: A clear pattern appears in this natural system.
rhetoric.definition: A concrete detail gives the reader something easy to see or imagine.
rhetoric.note: 用具体细节帮助读者看见场景，而不是只讲抽象道理。
rhetoric.effect: It makes the lesson easier to picture.
rhetoric.tryIt: The small key waits on the table.

articles.title: How Animals Hide in Plain Sight
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about animal camouflage, signals, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How Animals Hide in Plain Sight can be studied in forests, grasslands, deserts, and oceans. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. A movement or change can be a signal. It gives information about the system.
Next, color, shape, and behavior reduce visible contrast. This leads to an important result: an animal becomes harder to find.

A small detail helps explain the change. This natural system gradually takes a new form. The effect becomes clear after some time. A careful observer must prepare before the next step. An ordinary part of this natural system can become important.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, a changing background can reveal the animal.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because camouflage can protect hunters and prey.
a careful observer must take care of the smallest details.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

vocabulary.definition: a small fact or part of something
vocabulary.example: One detail changed the story.
vocabulary.usage: 常见搭配 important detail, in detail。

vocabulary.definition: slowly over a period of time
vocabulary.example: The sky gradually became darker.
vocabulary.usage: 用于描述并非突然发生的变化。

vocabulary.definition: a change caused by something else
vocabulary.example: Rain has an effect on the soil.
vocabulary.usage: have an effect on 表示“对……有影响”。

vocabulary.definition: to make something ready
vocabulary.example: We prepared for the long journey.
vocabulary.usage: prepare for + 名词；prepare to + 动词。

vocabulary.definition: normal and not unusual
vocabulary.example: It looked like an ordinary box.
vocabulary.usage: 可放在名词前描述日常事物。

vocabulary.definition: with careful attention
vocabulary.example: Look closely at the picture.
vocabulary.usage: 常修饰 look, watch, listen。

vocabulary.definition: having great value or effect
vocabulary.example: This is an important detail.
vocabulary.usage: important for/to ...。

vocabulary.definition: a description of events, real or imagined
vocabulary.example: The story has a clear ending.
vocabulary.usage: 可指虚构故事或新闻叙述。

vocabulary.definition: to make something clear or easy to understand
vocabulary.example: Can you explain the rule?
vocabulary.usage: explain sth to sb。

phrases.definition: to look after or deal with
phrases.example: a careful observer must take care of the smallest details.
phrases.usage: 后接人、动物、事物或任务。

grammar.target: When people look closely, they can see the difference.
grammar.definition: A when-clause introduces the condition or time for a general result.
grammar.note: when 从句使用一般现在时，主句用 can 表示通常可能看到的结果。
grammar.example: When the sun appears, we can see the colors clearly.

grammar.target: The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds.
grammar.definition: Because introduces the reason for an action or result.
grammar.note: because 引导原因从句，说明为什么会发生某事。
grammar.example: The path is closed because the river is high.

rhetoric.target: This natural system tells its story quietly.
rhetoric.definition: A non-human thing is given the human action of telling a story.
rhetoric.note: 把主题写成会讲故事的人，使描述更生动。
rhetoric.effect: 暗示细节需要读者主动观察才能被理解。
rhetoric.tryIt: The old house tells its story through its walls.

rhetoric.target: The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds.
rhetoric.definition: A contrast shows how two choices, ideas, or moments differ.
rhetoric.note: 通过对比突出变化或选择，让文章更有层次。
rhetoric.effect: It helps the reader notice the turning point.
rhetoric.tryIt: The room is quiet, but the decision is loud.

articles.title: How Ants Build a Trail
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about ant trails, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How Ants Build a Trail can be studied between a nest and a food source. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Scientists measure the system many times. One number is not enough.
Next, ants leave chemical signals that other ants can follow. This leads to an important result: a busy route forms without a leader drawing it.

An ordinary part of this natural system can become important. The solution depends on close attention. A careful observer chooses a different way instead. More than one answer is possible. A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, rain or a moved food source can break the trail.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because simple actions create group coordination.
In this way, the separate details form a complete idea.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: How Birds Find Their Way
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about bird migration, process, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How Birds Find Their Way can be studied between seasonal feeding and nesting areas. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. The steps happen in an order. One step helps the next step begin.
Next, birds combine body signals with the sun, stars, and landmarks. This leads to an important result: they travel long routes at useful times of year.

A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow. People discover something new about this natural system. A small detail helps explain the change. This natural system gradually takes a new form. The effect becomes clear after some time.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, storms and bright city lights can disturb the route.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because migration links distant habitats.
a careful observer can find out more by looking closely at this natural system.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: How City Trees Cool a Street
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about city trees, connections, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How City Trees Cool a Street can be studied beside roads and buildings. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. The system is connected to other living things and places.
Next, leaves provide shade and release water into the air. This leads to an important result: nearby surfaces and people become cooler.

A careful observer chooses a different way instead. More than one answer is possible. A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow. People discover something new about this natural system. A small detail helps explain the change.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, limited soil makes urban growth difficult.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because tree cover connects climate and public health.
a careful observer studies the evidence instead of guessing.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: How Fungi Clean a Forest
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about forest fungi, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How Fungi Clean a Forest can be studied in soil, dead wood, and living roots. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Scientists measure the system many times. One number is not enough.
Next, fine threads absorb material and break it into smaller parts. This leads to an important result: nutrients return to the ecosystem.

An ordinary part of this natural system can become important. The solution depends on close attention. A careful observer chooses a different way instead. More than one answer is possible. A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, dryness and pollution can interrupt the network.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because decomposition supports new growth.
In this way, the separate details form a complete idea.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: How Glaciers Move
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about glaciers, process, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: How Glaciers Move can be studied in cold mountains and polar regions. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. The steps happen in an order. One step helps the next step begin.
Next, years of snow become heavy ice that flows slowly downhill. This leads to an important result: ice reshapes valleys and stores fresh water.

A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow. People discover something new about this natural system. A small detail helps explain the change. This natural system gradually takes a new form. The effect becomes clear after some time.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, warming can make melting faster than new snowfall.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because glacier change affects rivers and sea level.
a careful observer can find out more by looking closely at this natural system.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about volcanoes, adaptation, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: What Happens Inside a Volcano can be studied above moving rock beneath Earth's surface. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Conditions can change. The system must respond in a new way.
Next, pressure pushes magma through cracks in the crust. This leads to an important result: lava, ash, and gas may reach the surface.

The effect becomes clear after some time. A careful observer must prepare before the next step. An ordinary part of this natural system can become important. The solution depends on close attention. A careful observer chooses a different way instead.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, trapped gas can make an eruption stronger.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because volcanoes create hazards and new land.
As soon as one condition changes, another response begins.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: Why Metal Rusts
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about rust, connections, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: Why Metal Rusts can be studied where iron meets water and oxygen. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. The system is connected to other living things and places.
Next, a chemical reaction slowly changes the metal surface. This leads to an important result: a reddish, weaker material forms.

A careful observer chooses a different way instead. More than one answer is possible. A careful observer remains patient when progress is slow. People discover something new about this natural system. A small detail helps explain the change.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, salt and moisture often make rust develop faster.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because coatings can slow the reaction.
a careful observer studies the evidence instead of guessing.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: Why Ocean Water Rises and Falls
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about ocean tides, signals, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: Why Ocean Water Rises and Falls can be studied along coastlines. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. A movement or change can be a signal. It gives information about the system.
Next, the gravity of the moon and sun pulls on ocean water. This leads to an important result: water levels rise and fall in a regular cycle.

A small detail helps explain the change. This natural system gradually takes a new form. The effect becomes clear after some time. A careful observer must prepare before the next step. An ordinary part of this natural system can become important.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, coast shape changes local timing.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because tides affect coastal animals and boats.
a careful observer must take care of the smallest details.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.
This natural system tells its story quietly.

articles.title: Why the Moon Changes Shape
articles.levelFocus: everyday description, sequence, and simple cause and effect
articles.dek: A A2 science explainer about moon phases, adaptation, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NASA Science
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for A2.
body: Why the Moon Changes Shape can be studied in the night sky across a month. The process begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions. Conditions can change. The system must respond in a new way.
Next, the moon moves around Earth while sunlight reaches half of it. This leads to an important result: people see different portions of the bright half.

The effect becomes clear after some time. A careful observer must prepare before the next step. An ordinary part of this natural system can become important. The solution depends on close attention. A careful observer chooses a different way instead.
Conditions do not always stay the same. For example, clouds can hide the view but not change the phase.
The system responds because observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. Scientists compare many observations to understand the change.
The topic also connects to a wider world because the phases create a predictable calendar.
As soon as one condition changes, another response begins.
When people look closely, they can see the difference.

articles.title: How an Electricity Grid Balances Power
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about electricity grids, adaptation, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: Electricity grids can be studied between generators, wires, storage, and users. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. A response becomes especially important when the surrounding conditions no longer remain stable.
At the center of the system, operators match production with changing demand. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

The final approach proves effective. Understanding this natural system can require time. Several conditions influence what happens. A better approach begins with observation. The decision has an unexpected consequence.

The immediate result is that electrical energy reaches homes and businesses reliably. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: variable wind and solar power require flexible control. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
As a result, the next stage develops differently.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

vocabulary.definition: successful in producing the intended result
vocabulary.example: This is an effective way to save water.
vocabulary.usage: effective way/method/solution 是常见搭配。

vocabulary.definition: to need something in order to happen or work

vocabulary.definition: a way of dealing with a task or problem

vocabulary.example: Every choice has a consequence.
vocabulary.usage: 常用 consequence of，语气通常比 result 更强。

vocabulary.example: Conditions changed during the test.
vocabulary.usage: 常用复数表示整体环境。

vocabulary.definition: an answer or reaction
vocabulary.example: Her response changed the plan.

phrases.usage: 通常放句首，连接原因和结果。

grammar.note: 条件从句说明可能发生的情况，may 表示结果并非绝对。

rhetoric.tryIt: The journey was short, but its effect was lasting.

articles.title: How Bees Give Directions
articles.levelFocus: process explanation and spatial language
articles.dek: Inside a dark hive, a moving bee can tell thousands of workers where to find flowers.
articles.sourceName: Nobel Prize: Karl von Frisch and the dance language of bees
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established research on honey-bee communication.
body: A honey bee leaves its colony and flies across fields in search of food. When it finds flowers rich in nectar, it drinks a little and returns to the hive. The bee does not keep the discovery secret. Instead, it performs a dance that helps other workers find the same place.

The most important movement is called the waggle run. The bee moves forward while shaking its body, then turns and repeats the action. The direction of the run gives information about direction outside. Because the hive is dark, the bee uses gravity as a guide. An upward run may mean that the flowers are toward the sun; a run at an angle points to a different route.

The dance also communicates distance. The longer the waggle run lasts, the farther the food is from the hive. Other bees touch the dancer and follow her movements. They also notice the smell of the flowers on her body. Together, movement and scent pass on a surprisingly detailed message.

The dance is not a set of human words. Weather, wind, and the movement of the sun can change the journey, so the bees must adjust. Yet the system allows a single worker to guide many others toward a valuable source of food.
The hive is dark, but the dance is a map. It shows that communication does not always require a voice. Sometimes a body moving through space can carry direction, distance, and news.

vocabulary.definition: a group of insects of the same type living together
vocabulary.note: 这里指共同生活的整个蜂群。
vocabulary.example: A colony of ants lived under the stone.
vocabulary.chinese: 这里指共同生活的整个蜂群。
vocabulary.usage: 结合正文中的语境和上方例句使用，注意词性及常见搭配。

vocabulary.definition: a sweet liquid produced by flowers
vocabulary.note: 蜜蜂从花朵中采集的含糖液体。
vocabulary.example: Butterflies feed on flower nectar.

vocabulary.usage: 结合正文中的语境和上方例句使用，注意词性及常见搭配。

vocabulary.definition: the force that pulls things toward the earth
vocabulary.example: Bees use gravity as a guide.
vocabulary.usage: 不可数名词。

grammar.target: The longer the waggle run lasts, the farther the food is from the hive.

grammar.example: The faster you walk, the sooner you arrive.

grammar.note: 从句可以补充对比、时间或背景，使句子信息更完整。
grammar.example: When the evidence changes, the decision changes.

rhetoric.definition: The dance is described as a map because both provide directions.

rhetoric.effect: 结尾把复杂科学过程压缩成一个容易记住的画面。

articles.title: How DNA Stores Instructions
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about DNA, process, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: DNA can be studied inside the cells of living organisms. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. The order of the stages matters because each stage creates the conditions for the next one.

At the center of the system, sequences of chemical bases carry information used to build molecules. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

A reader can observe a change in this natural system. The evidence appears in several small details. This natural system produces a clear response. The situation develops gradually rather than at once. The final approach proves effective.

The immediate result is that cells copy and read instructions for growth and repair. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: copying changes can alter an instruction. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
A full explanation must take into account the conditions around this natural system.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

vocabulary.definition: to watch or examine something carefully
vocabulary.example: Scientists observe how the material changes.
vocabulary.usage: 可接名词，也可用 observe how/that 引出观察结果。

vocabulary.definition: facts or signs that support an idea
vocabulary.example: The marks provide evidence of use.
vocabulary.usage: 不可数名词；常用 evidence of/for。

vocabulary.definition: an answer or reaction to something
vocabulary.example: Her response surprised the group.
vocabulary.usage: response to + 事件或问题。

vocabulary.definition: slowly and by small amounts
vocabulary.example: The town gradually changed.
vocabulary.usage: 适合描述过程、趋势和人物变化。

vocabulary.definition: successful in producing the intended result
vocabulary.example: This is an effective way to save water.
vocabulary.usage: effective way/method/solution 是常见搭配。

vocabulary.definition: the facts or situation that affect how something happens
vocabulary.example: Conditions changed during the test.
vocabulary.usage: 常用复数表示整体环境。

vocabulary.definition: more than two but not many
vocabulary.example: Several people joined the work.
vocabulary.usage: 后接复数名词。

vocabulary.definition: happening more than once
vocabulary.example: Repeated practice helps memory.
vocabulary.usage: 由 repeat + -ed 构成。

vocabulary.definition: last in a series
vocabulary.example: The final choice was hard.
vocabulary.usage: final answer/decision。

phrases.definition: to consider a fact when deciding
phrases.example: A full explanation must take into account the conditions around this natural system.
phrases.usage: account 前通常使用 take；后接需要考虑的因素。

phrases.definition: used to show a choice or contrast
phrases.example: It invites thought rather than quick judgment.
phrases.usage: 用于对比两个选择。

grammar.target: If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
grammar.definition: If + present form introduces a possible condition and result.
grammar.note: 条件从句说明可能发生的情况，may 表示结果并非绝对。
grammar.example: If the temperature falls, the water may freeze.

grammar.target: The situation develops gradually rather than at once.

grammar.note: 从句可以补充对比、时间或背景，使句子信息更完整。

articles.title: How Food Webs Hold an Ecosystem Together
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about food webs, connections, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: Food webs can be studied among producers, consumers, and decomposers. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. No part exists alone; energy, material, and information move between this system and its surroundings.

At the center of the system, energy and material move through many connected feeding relationships. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

The decision has an unexpected consequence. The method becomes more reliable with repeated checks. A reader can observe a change in this natural system. The evidence appears in several small details. This natural system produces a clear response.
The immediate result is that changes in one population affect several others. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: the loss of a key species can reorganize connections. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
A new pattern appears in response to the changing conditions.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

articles.title: How Rivers Shape the Land
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about river erosion, signals, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: River erosion can be studied from steep headwaters to broad valleys. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Changes in movement, timing, shape, or position can act as signals about the state of the system.
At the center of the system, moving water lifts, carries, and deposits rock and soil. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

This natural system produces a clear response. The situation develops gradually rather than at once. The final approach proves effective. Understanding this natural system can require time. Several conditions influence what happens.
The immediate result is that channels, bends, floodplains, and deltas develop. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: dams and extreme rain can change the pattern. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
Several conditions play a role in the final result.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. A response becomes especially important when the surrounding conditions no longer remain stable.
At the center of the system, cells detect patterns linked to damage or infection. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

The final approach proves effective. Understanding this natural system can require time. Several conditions influence what happens. A better approach begins with observation. The decision has an unexpected consequence.

The immediate result is that different defenses remove threats and remember some of them. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: the system must avoid attacking healthy tissue. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
As a result, the next stage develops differently.

articles.title: How Weather Forecasts Are Made
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about weather forecasting, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: Weather forecasting can be studied across the atmosphere and computer models. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Repeated measurement separates a lasting pattern from an event that happened only once.

At the center of the system, instruments measure air while models calculate possible changes. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

Several conditions influence what happens. A better approach begins with observation. The decision has an unexpected consequence. The method becomes more reliable with repeated checks. A reader can observe a change in this natural system.

The immediate result is that forecasters estimate future conditions and uncertainty. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: small measurement errors can grow over time. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The system must deal with change over time.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

articles.title: Why Antibiotics Do Not Kill Viruses
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about antibiotics, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institutes of Health education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: Antibiotics can be studied where medicines meet different kinds of infection. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Repeated measurement separates a lasting pattern from an event that happened only once.

At the center of the system, antibiotics target structures or processes found in bacteria. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

Several conditions influence what happens. A better approach begins with observation. The decision has an unexpected consequence. The method becomes more reliable with repeated checks. A reader can observe a change in this natural system.
The immediate result is that many bacterial infections can be controlled. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: unnecessary use can also favor resistant bacteria. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The system must deal with change over time.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

vocabulary.definition: to affect how something develops or behaves
vocabulary.example: Weather can influence the result.
vocabulary.usage: influence + 人/决定/结果，不需要介词 on。

vocabulary.definition: a way of dealing with a task or problem
vocabulary.example: We tried a different approach to the problem.
vocabulary.usage: an approach to + 名词或 -ing。

vocabulary.definition: a result, especially one caused by an action
vocabulary.example: Every choice has a consequence.
vocabulary.usage: 常用 consequence of，语气通常比 result 更强。

vocabulary.definition: able to be trusted or depended on
vocabulary.example: Use a reliable source of information.
vocabulary.usage: 常修饰 source, method, person, result。

vocabulary.definition: to watch or examine something carefully
vocabulary.example: Scientists observe how the material changes.
vocabulary.usage: 可接名词，也可用 observe how/that 引出观察结果。

vocabulary.definition: the facts or situation that affect how something happens
vocabulary.example: Conditions changed during the test.
vocabulary.usage: 常用复数表示整体环境。

vocabulary.definition: an answer or reaction
vocabulary.example: Her response changed the plan.
vocabulary.usage: response to ... 表示“对……的回应”。

vocabulary.definition: more than two but not many
vocabulary.example: Several people joined the work.
vocabulary.usage: 后接复数名词。

vocabulary.definition: happening more than once
vocabulary.example: Repeated practice helps memory.
vocabulary.usage: 由 repeat + -ed 构成。

phrases.definition: to take action about a problem
phrases.example: The system must deal with change over time.
phrases.usage: 后接问题、情况或主题。

phrases.definition: across a period, not all at once
phrases.example: Small changes appear over time.
phrases.usage: 常用于描述慢慢发生的变化。

grammar.target: If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
grammar.definition: If + present form introduces a possible condition and result.
grammar.note: 条件从句说明可能发生的情况，may 表示结果并非绝对。
grammar.example: If the temperature falls, the water may freeze.

grammar.target: Antibiotics can be studied where medicines meet different kinds of infection.
grammar.definition: A clause can show contrast, timing, or the condition around the main idea.
grammar.note: 从句可以补充对比、时间或背景，使句子信息更完整。
grammar.example: When the evidence changes, the decision changes.

rhetoric.target: The change is small, but its meaning is large.
rhetoric.definition: Two opposing qualities are placed together for emphasis.
rhetoric.note: small 与 large 形成直接对比。
rhetoric.effect: 突出细微现象可能具有重要意义。
rhetoric.tryIt: The journey was short, but its effect was lasting.

rhetoric.target: A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it.
rhetoric.definition: Cause and effect connects actions with consequences.
rhetoric.note: 因果链让读者看出一个选择如何带来后续结果。
rhetoric.effect: It turns a list of events into a meaningful sequence.
rhetoric.tryIt: One missed signal changes the whole plan.

articles.title: Why Continents Move
articles.levelFocus: connected paragraphs, clear reasons, and familiar abstract ideas
articles.dek: A B1 science explainer about plate tectonics, signals, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B1.
body: Plate tectonics can be studied across Earth's outer shell. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Changes in movement, timing, shape, or position can act as signals about the state of the system.
At the center of the system, large plates move slowly over hotter material below. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

This natural system produces a clear response. The situation develops gradually rather than at once. The final approach proves effective. Understanding this natural system can require time. Several conditions influence what happens.

The immediate result is that continents shift and mountains, trenches, and faults form. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: the movement is too slow to observe directly in daily life. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
Several conditions play a role in the final result.
If we ignore the context, we may miss the point.
The change is small, but its meaning is large.

articles.title: How Algorithms Learn Patterns
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about machine learning, signals, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Machine learning can be studied inside systems trained on many examples. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Changes in movement, timing, shape, or position can act as signals about the state of the system.

At the center of the system, an algorithm adjusts internal values to reduce prediction errors. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

One assumption shapes the first explanation. Ultimately, the outcome depends on several connected choices. A careful observer must adapt when the conditions change. Every solution must work within a constraint. Several details reinforce the same conclusion.
The immediate result is that the model can classify or estimate new cases. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: biased examples can produce unreliable results. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that performance depends on data and evaluation choices. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
Small differences can give rise to a larger effect.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

vocabulary.definition: something accepted as true without proof
vocabulary.example: The experiment challenged an old assumption.
vocabulary.usage: 常见搭配 make/challenge an assumption。

vocabulary.definition: finally, after considering everything
vocabulary.example: Ultimately, the choice belongs to the community.
vocabulary.usage: 常放句首或主要结论之前。

vocabulary.definition: to change in order to suit new conditions
vocabulary.example: Plants adapt to dry conditions.
vocabulary.usage: adapt to + 环境；adapt something for + 用途。

vocabulary.definition: a limit that controls what can be done
vocabulary.example: Cost was the main constraint.
vocabulary.usage: 常与 time, cost, space 等搭配。

vocabulary.definition: to make an idea, structure, or feeling stronger
vocabulary.example: Repeated images reinforce the main idea.
vocabulary.usage: reinforce an idea/pattern/message。

vocabulary.definition: a statement that makes something clear
vocabulary.example: The explanation connects cause and effect.
vocabulary.usage: explanation of/for ...。

vocabulary.definition: larger in range or influence
vocabulary.example: The issue has a wider effect.
vocabulary.usage: 比较级，用于扩展视角。

vocabulary.definition: not joined or not the same
vocabulary.example: These are separate problems.
vocabulary.usage: 可作形容词或动词。

vocabulary.definition: having many connected parts
vocabulary.example: A complex system needs careful study.
vocabulary.usage: 常修饰 system/problem/process。

phrases.definition: to cause something to happen
phrases.example: Small differences can give rise to a larger effect.
phrases.usage: 通常用于正式说明原因和结果。

phrases.definition: is decided or changed by something
phrases.example: The answer depends on context.
phrases.usage: 后接名词或 wh- 从句。

grammar.target: Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
grammar.definition: Although introduces a fact that contrasts with the main clause.
grammar.note: although 引出让步信息，主句给出更重要或相反的判断。
grammar.example: Although the method is fast, it is not always reliable.

rhetoric.target: This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.
rhetoric.definition: The subject is described as a bridge because it connects ideas.
rhetoric.note: 用 bridge 表示主题在多个因素之间建立联系。
rhetoric.effect: 把系统关系转化为读者熟悉的空间形象。
rhetoric.tryIt: Shared language became a bridge between the groups.

rhetoric.target: The wider connection is that performance depends on data and evaluation choices.
rhetoric.definition: Analytical framing places a detail inside a wider explanation.
rhetoric.note: 把细节放进更大的解释框架中，帮助读者理解复杂关系。
rhetoric.effect: It moves the paragraph from description toward argument.
rhetoric.tryIt: A small choice reveals a wider system.

articles.sourceName: NASA Science
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Exoplanet detection can be studied in tiny changes within light from distant stars. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Changes in movement, timing, shape, or position can act as signals about the state of the system.

At the center of the system, telescopes measure dimming or stellar motion caused by an orbiting planet. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

One assumption shapes the first explanation. Ultimately, the outcome depends on several connected choices. A careful observer must adapt when the conditions change. Every solution must work within a constraint. Several details reinforce the same conclusion.

The immediate result is that researchers infer worlds that cannot usually be seen directly. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: noise and other stellar activity can imitate a signal. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.

The wider connection is that multiple methods reveal size, orbit, and sometimes atmosphere. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
Small differences can give rise to a larger effect.

articles.title: How Carbon Dating Reads the Past
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about radiocarbon dating, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Radiocarbon dating can be studied in once-living material. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Repeated measurement separates a lasting pattern from an event that happened only once.

At the center of the system, scientists measure the remaining amount of a radioactive carbon isotope. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

Several details reinforce the same conclusion. A wider perspective changes how we understand this natural system. The wider consequence may not appear immediately. A subtle change can alter the whole experience. A small action can have a significant effect on this natural system.
The immediate result is that a probability range estimates when an organism died. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: contamination and calibration affect accuracy. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that dating connects objects to environmental and human history. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
To some extent, the result depends on perspective.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

articles.title: How Earthquake Early Warning Works
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about earthquake early warning, connections, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Earthquake early warning can be studied between ground sensors, communication networks, and threatened cities. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. No part exists alone; energy, material, and information move between this system and its surroundings.

At the center of the system, systems detect fast seismic signals and estimate stronger shaking still on its way. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

The wider consequence may not appear immediately. A subtle change can alter the whole experience. A small action can have a significant effect on this natural system. People interpret the same detail in different ways. One assumption shapes the first explanation.
The immediate result is that alerts create seconds for automatic and human action. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: nearby locations may receive little or no warning time. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that warning turns measurement speed into practical preparation. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
In the long run, repeated choices reshape the outcome.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

vocabulary.usage: 常修饰 difference, effect, change, signal。

body: Black-hole observation can be studied around matter and light near an invisible object. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. A response becomes especially important when the surrounding conditions no longer remain stable.

At the center of the system, scientists measure radiation, orbital motion, and gravitational effects. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

A careful observer must adapt when the conditions change. Every solution must work within a constraint. Several details reinforce the same conclusion. A wider perspective changes how we understand this natural system. The wider consequence may not appear immediately.

The immediate result is that evidence reveals mass concentrated in a very small region. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: different explanations must be compared carefully. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that indirect observation can test extreme physics. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
The contrast can draw attention to a hidden detail.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

articles.title: Life Without Sunlight at Deep-Sea Vents
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about deep-sea vent ecosystems, adaptation, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.

A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. A response becomes especially important when the surrounding conditions no longer remain stable.
At the center of the system, microbes use chemical energy instead of sunlight. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

A careful observer must adapt when the conditions change. Every solution must work within a constraint. Several details reinforce the same conclusion. A wider perspective changes how we understand this natural system. The wider consequence may not appear immediately.
The immediate result is that dense communities survive in complete darkness. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: vents are unstable and widely separated. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that chemosynthesis expands the known foundations of food webs. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.

articles.title: What CRISPR Changes in a Cell
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about CRISPR gene editing, measurement, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: Smithsonian science education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: CRISPR gene editing can be studied inside selected regions of DNA. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. Repeated measurement separates a lasting pattern from an event that happened only once.

At the center of the system, a guide molecule directs a protein to cut a chosen sequence. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

Several details reinforce the same conclusion. A wider perspective changes how we understand this natural system. The wider consequence may not appear immediately. A subtle change can alter the whole experience. A small action can have a significant effect on this natural system.
The immediate result is that cell repair can disable or alter genetic information. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: unintended edits and ethical choices require scrutiny. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that the tool supports research and possible treatments. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
To some extent, the result depends on perspective.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

articles.title: Where Ocean Microplastics Travel
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about microplastics, connections, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: NOAA and USGS education resources
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Microplastics can be studied from rivers and coasts into ocean currents and food webs. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. No part exists alone; energy, material, and information move between this system and its surroundings.

At the center of the system, small plastic particles move, sink, break apart, and enter organisms. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

The wider consequence may not appear immediately. A subtle change can alter the whole experience. A small action can have a significant effect on this natural system. People interpret the same detail in different ways. One assumption shapes the first explanation.
The immediate result is that pollution spreads far from its original source. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: measurement remains difficult across environments. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that size and chemistry affect biological risk. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
In the long run, repeated choices reshape the outcome.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.

articles.title: Why Renewable Energy Needs Storage
articles.levelFocus: detailed explanation, contrast, and implied meaning
articles.dek: A B2 science explainer about energy storage, process, and connected systems.
articles.sourceName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
articles.adaptationNote: Written for English Reading Lab from established educational material; graded for B2.
body: Energy storage can be studied between variable electricity production and demand. A useful explanation begins with energy, material, and information from the surrounding conditions, because no natural process happens without conditions around it. The order of the stages matters because each stage creates the conditions for the next one.
At the center of the system, batteries and other systems hold energy for later use. This activity is not a single event. It consists of connected stages, each of which changes what can happen next.

A small action can have a significant effect on this natural system. People interpret the same detail in different ways. One assumption shapes the first explanation. Ultimately, the outcome depends on several connected choices. A careful observer must adapt when the conditions change.

The immediate result is that supply can remain stable when wind or sunlight changes. Observers can compare places, times, or individuals to identify which conditions make the result stronger or weaker.

Change introduces a challenge: cost, materials, safety, and efficiency create trade-offs. In response, observers compare repeated evidence to understand how the system responds. The response has limits, however, and it does not guarantee that the system will remain unchanged.
Measurement matters because appearances alone can be misleading. Repeated observations reveal timing, variation, and unusual events that one brief visit would miss.
The wider connection is that different storage times require different technologies. For this reason, studying one process can illuminate relationships that extend far beyond the original site.
In contrast, a wider view reveals several connected causes.
Although the first explanation seems simple, the wider system is more complex.
This natural system becomes a bridge between separate parts of the explanation.
